book.title: Introduction to Mathematical Structures
book.author: Steven Galovich
book.tagline: A working companion — proofs, tables, and interactive graphs, kept and extended chapter by chapter.

blocks.html: Galovich states the division algorithm and proves the existence half by mathematical induction. The version proved here fixes $a$ and $b$ as positive integers — and that restriction does real work. It lets the proof run as a plain forward induction on $a$ from a base case, and it lets the remainder bound be written $0 \le r < b$ with no absolute values. Everything below is the proof, plus the structure the “easy and obvious” framing tends to skip.

blocks.label: Theorem 1.3.6
blocks.html: For positive integers $a$ and $b$, there exist integers $q$ and $r$ such that $$a = bq + r, \qquad 0 \le r < b.$$

blocks.title: On the bound
blocks.html: The remainder bound is $0 \le r \le b-1$, equivalently $0 \le r < b$. The value $r = b-1$ must be legal — it is exactly the case that forces a carry into the quotient in the inductive step. The fully general theorem allows any $b \neq 0$ with $0 \le r < |b|$; forcing $a, b > 0$ removes the negative branches and the absolute value.

Existence, by induction on $a$ with $b$ held fixed. Let $S(a)$ be the claim that integers $q, r$ exist with $a = bq + r$ and $0 \le r < b$.

Base ($a = 1$). If $b = 1$, then $1 = 1\cdot 1 + 0$. If $b \ge 2$, then $1 = b\cdot 0 + 1$ with $0 \le 1 < b$. Either way $S(1)$ holds.

Step. Assume $S(a)$, so $a = bq + r$ with $0 \le r < b$. Then $a + 1 = bq + (r+1)$. Since $0 \le r \le b-1$, there are two cases:

  • If $r + 1 < b$: take $q' = q$ and $r' = r+1$. Then $a+1 = bq' + r'$ with $0 \le r' < b$.
  • If $r + 1 = b$ (that is, $r = b-1$): then $a+1 = bq + b = b(q+1) + 0$, so $q' = q+1$ and $r' = 0$.

In both cases $S(a+1)$ holds, so by induction $S(a)$ holds for every $a \ge 1$. $\blacksquare$

blocks.title: Uniqueness (separate, non-inductive)
blocks.html: If $bq_1 + r_1 = bq_2 + r_2$ then $b \mid (r_1 - r_2)$ while $|r_1 - r_2| < b$, which forces $r_1 = r_2$ and hence $q_1 = q_2$. So the $(q, r)$ produced above is the only one.

blocks.title: Which variable can you induct on?
blocks.html: Both $a$ and $b$ are universally quantified, so either is a candidate for induction — but only $a$ carries a usable recurrence. Incrementing $a$ by one bumps the remainder by one, with a clean carry into $q$. Incrementing $b$ has no step-to-step relationship: $q$ and $r$ jump around irregularly. So the move is to universally generalize on $b$ (“let $b$ be arbitrary but fixed”) and induct on $a$.

blocks.caption: Fix $b=4$, walk $a$. The remainder $r$ sawtooths $0,1,2,3$ with period $b$; the quotient $q$ is a staircase that steps up exactly when $r$ resets. The two induction-step cases made physical: three-in-four steps are the easy case ($r{+}1<b$), every fourth is the carry ($r=b{-}1 \to 0$, $q{+}1$).

blocks.html:
Relaxing the positivity constraint

Keep the convention $0 \le r < |b|$ — the remainder stays non-negative, so $-1 = 4\cdot(-1) + 3$, never $4\cdot 0 - 1$. Then negative $a$ just extends the sawtooth leftward (same teeth), and negative $b$ leaves $r$ identical while mirroring the quotient staircase. The theorem was sign-agnostic all along; only the forward induction needed positivity.

blocks.caption: Shared sawtooth, two mirror-image staircases. $r$ depends only on $|b|$, so it is the same for $b=4$ and $b=-4$; the sign information lives entirely in $q$. At $a=10$: $r=2$, with $q=2$ (for $b=4$) or $q=-2$ (for $b=-4$).

blocks.title: What actually breaks
blocks.html: Nothing in the theorem breaks under negative $a$ or $b$. What breaks is the proof method: forward induction needs a base case and only walks upward, so it cannot reach $a < 0$. Covering negatives needs a second downward induction, or the well-ordering proof (take the least non-negative element of $\{a - bk : k \in \mathbb{Z}\}$), which is sign-blind and gets the whole integer line at once. The only genuine casualty is $b = 0$, where $0 \le r < 0$ is empty.

blocks.title: Reading the deconstruction table
blocks.html: The $r=0$ rows are exactly the divisors of $10$ — the algorithm silently encodes $b \mid a \iff r = 0$. Once $b > a$ the only legal answer is $q=0,\ r=a$ (uniqueness forces it), giving the constant tail. This table fixes $a$ and walks $b$ — the direction you cannot induct on — which is why the $q$ and $r$ columns look irregular.

$a = bq + r$ as a coordinate lattice

Treat $b$ as a parameter and plot the two real degrees of freedom $q$ and $r$ as axes, writing $a = bq + r$ inside each cell. The pair $(q, r)$ becomes a coordinate system for the integers, and the division algorithm is the inverse lookup: given $a$, find its cell.

blocks.caption: Walk to column $q=2$, up to row $r=2$, read the cell: $10$. Up one row $= +1$; right one column $= +b$. The bottom row ($r=0$) holds the multiples of $b$. Extend the grid forever and every integer appears exactly once — that bijection is the entire content of the theorem (existence + uniqueness), and the height being exactly $b$ rows is the bound $0 \le r < b$.

The dependency, as a sum. The staircase $bq$ is the best approach to the diagonal $y=a$ using whole jumps of $b$; the sawtooth $r$ is the leftover gap. At every $a$: $bq + r = a$. At $a=10$: $8 + 2 = 10$.

blocks.title: Why this graph reads busier
blocks.html: The $y$-axis here is a shared value axis (same units as $a$), carrying input and outputs together: the gray diagonal is $a$ plotted against itself, while $bq$ and $r$ are the outputs of dividing. The cleaner sawtooth graph keeps the $x$-axis as the input $a$ and the $y$-axis as outputs only. This one trades that cleanliness to show the conservation law $bq + r = a$ directly.

$y = mx + b$ was hiding inside it

Read $a = bq + r$ as slope-intercept form with $q$ as the variable: $a = b\cdot q + r$. The divisor is the slope, the remainder is the intercept. Each $r = 0, 1, \dots, b-1$ gives a parallel line of slope $b$.

blocks.caption: Four parallel lines, slope $4$, intercepts $0,1,2,3$. The integer points on these four lines together hit every integer exactly once — the sawtooth riding up a ladder of parallel lines. $a=10$ sits on the $r=2$ line at $q=2$. Between consecutive lattice points each line rises $b$ over a run of $1$: congruent right triangles, the “triangular” structure made literal.

blocks.html:
Swapping $b$ and $q$ — does $r$ still cycle?

Because $bq$ is a product, you can equally read $a = q\cdot b + r$ with $b$ on the $x$-axis and $q$ as the (fixed) slope. Fixing $q$ then gives parallel lines indexed by $r$. But watch what happens to the cycle.

blocks.caption: Fix $q=2$: four parallel lines of slope $2$, one per remainder. The slice at $b=4$ crosses them at $a=8,9,10,11$, reading $r=0,1,2,3$ once — then $a=12$ falls off, because $12 = 4\cdot 3 + 0$ jumps to a new family with a new slope. Fixing $q$ shows exactly one period of $r$; the repeat is driven entirely by $q$ ticking up.

blocks.title: The asymmetry that defines the modulus
blocks.html: $a = bq + r$ is symmetric in $b$ and $q$ — you can make either one the slope. But the constraint $0 \le r < b$ is not symmetric: it bounds the remainder by $b$, never by $q$. That broken symmetry is the whole reason $b$ (not $q$) is the modulus, why the clean period-$b$ cycle lives in the $a$-direction at fixed $b$, and why a perfectly legal $b \leftrightarrow q$ swap quietly costs you the cycle. With $b$ on the axis the count of legal remainders grows with $b$, so the constant-height strip opens into a wedge — the triangle again, now as the shadow of $r < b$.

blocks.title: Two directions, one theorem
blocks.html:
Analysis (sawtooth / lattice): input $a$ on $x$, outputs $q, r$ on $y$ — given a number, extract its coordinates. This is the division algorithm.
Synthesis (parallel lines): input $q$ on $x$, output $a = bq + r$ on $y$ — given a quotient, build the number. The two are inverses, and the same theorem seen from three angles: dynamics (the sawtooth), symmetry (the signed mirror), and structure (the lattice and its ruled family of parallel lines).
